Tal cual. Lo que llamamos IA es, esencialmente, un modelo de lenguaje. Un productor de refritos.
Hay otros usos de IA (plegado de proteínas, por ejemplo), que no se basan en LLMs y sí tienen un cierto futuro... y si algún día conseguimos una IA generalista, otro gallo cantará. Pero lo de ahora, meh.

Wikipedia es uno de los mayores perjudicados, pero en general todas las webs de contenido (periódicos, stackoverflow, hasta reddit) ha visto su tráfico disminuido con la IA en las búsquedas. Para que vas a salir de google si lo tienes todo ahí? (tenga la calidad que tenga, claro...)

- Replace everything with much better apps.

Microsoft 365 Copilot Declarative Agents Are Getting Smarter with GPT‑5.2

external.description: We’re continuing to improve the intelligence behind Microsoft 365 Copilot Declarative Agents. As part of this effort, Declarative Agents are being updated to use GPT‑5.2, a newer large language model designed to deliver higher‑quality responses across complex agent scenarios. This update focuses on improving how agents reason, plan, and produce structured outputs - helping them perform better on the tasks that matter most to your organization. Note: This update is gradually rolling out to all the tenants worldwide. Rollout is expected to be completed by end of March 2026. What’s new with GPT‑5.2? GPT‑5.2 brings meaningful quality improvements for agent experiences that involve: Reasoning and multi‑step tasks Better handling of workflows that require planning, iteration, or decision‑making across multiple steps. Tool usage and orchestration More reliable tool calling and coordination when agents interact with data sources, APIs, or enterprise tools. Document analysis Improved comprehension and synthesis of longer or more complex documents. Structured, well‑formatted outputs Clearer tables, summaries, and formatted responses that are easier to consume and act on. What does this mean for customers? Because the underlying model is being upgraded, you may notice: Improved response quality across many agent scenarios Subtle changes in behavior for some prompts or workflows, especially those that rely on reasoning, formatting, or tool usage These changes are expected and reflect the model’s improved capabilities. In most cases, agents should perform the same or better than before. How will this update roll out? The GPT‑5.2 update is rolling out gradually. Not all users within a tenant will receive the update at the same time. This phased approach helps ensure a smooth transition and allows us to monitor quality closely as the update expands. What should admins and agent builders do? No configuration changes are required to receive this update. However, we recommend a few simple steps to ensure a smooth experience: For admins Inform agent builders that a model update is rolling out so they can plan light validation. For agent builders Validate your most important scenarios Run a quick check on your top workflows (for example, your top 5–10 prompts or use cases). Confirm expectations are still met Pay attention to accuracy, tone, reasoning, and formatting. Adjust agent instructions if needed Small prompt or instruction refinements can help agents take full advantage of GPT‑5.2’s capabilities. Share feedback to help us improve Your feedback plays a critical role in improving Copilot experiences. If you notice issues or unexpected behavior: Use thumbs up / thumbs down on agent responses Include #GPT52 in your feedback description to help us route it quickly This helps our teams identify patterns, address issues faster, and continue improving quality. Looking ahead GPT‑5.2 is another step forward in making Copilot Declarative Agents more capable, reliable, and helpful for real‑world work. We’ll continue to evolve the models behind Copilot while prioritizing quality, transparency, and customer trust. Stay tuned for more updates as we continue to enhance Copilot experiences across Microsoft 365.

Tool usage and orchestration More reliable tool calling and coordination when agents interact with data sources, APIs, or enterprise tools. Document analysis Improved comprehension and synthesis of longer or more complex documents. Structured, well‑formatted outputs Clearer tables, summaries, and formatted responses that are easier to consume and act on. What does this mean for customers? Because the underlying model is being upgraded, you may notice: Improved response quality across many agent scenarios Subtle changes in behavior for some prompts or workflows, especially those that rely on reasoning, formatting, or tool usage These changes are expected and reflect the model’s improved capabilities. In most cases, agents should perform the same or better than before. How will this update roll out? The GPT‑5.2 update is rolling out gradually. Not all users within a tenant will receive the update at the same time. This phased approach helps ensure a smooth transition and allows us to monitor quality closely as the update expands. What should admins and agent builders do? No configuration changes are required to receive this update. However, we recommend a few simple steps to ensure a smooth experience: For admins Inform agent builders that a model update is rolling out so they can plan light validation. For agent builders Validate your most important scenarios Run a quick check on your top workflows (for example, your top 5–10 prompts or use cases). Confirm expectations are still met Pay attention to accuracy, tone, reasoning, and formatting. Adjust agent instructions if needed Small prompt or instruction refinements can help agents take full advantage of GPT‑5.2’s capabilities. Share feedback to help us improve Your feedback plays a critical role in improving Copilot experiences. If you notice issues or unexpected behavior: Use thumbs up / thumbs down on agent responses Include #GPT52 in your feedback description to help us route it quickly This helps our teams identify patterns, address issues faster, and continue improving quality. Looking ahead GPT‑5.2 is another step forward in making Copilot Declarative Agents more capable, reliable, and helpful for real‑world work. We’ll continue to evolve the models behind Copilot while prioritizing quality, transparency, and customer trust. Stay tuned for more updates as we continue to enhance Copilot experiences across Microsoft 365.
external.title: Microsoft 365 Copilot Declarative Agents Are Getting Smarter with GPT‑5.2

record.bridgyOriginalText:
@eatyourgreens
I don't use Copilot but its point is not entirely accurate or complete.

It's incomplete because it doesn't mention `title` attributes not being reliably available for touchscreen users.
It's inaccurate because for assistive technology (screen reader) users, `title` attributes may be reliably available. The attribute isn't favored but is included in accessible name and description computation rules.

Copilot's "pairing it with visible text" suggestion is the right solution for sighted keyboard and touchscreen users.

If the `title` is on an element with no role, yes, screen readers will very likely ignore it (even though browser DevTools will still show it in an accessibility tree rendering), macOS VoiceOver does.

But if `role="img"` is added, as Copilot correctly suggests, the `title` can be computed as the element's name so that would be sufficient.

Adding `aria-label` while keeping the `title` with the same value means some users will get the same text twice, as the name and again as the description (some screen readers have heuristics to not say a description if it's the same as the name).

external.title: Original post on c.im

Meier Galblum Haigh @mghaigh.bsky.social and Kenrya Rankin @kenrya.bsky.social “unpack the realities of AI, from access tools to authorship, labor, and institutional policy" on a new podcast episode from Grantmakers in the Arts. Listen now & read the transcript at linktr.ee/DisabilityCultureLab.

images.alt: Background is an eggshell-colored square with a red border. At the top is the Disability Culture Lab logo in teal. Below the logo are two square pictures. On the left is Meier Galblum Haigh, a white trans-masc nonbinary person with short cropped dark curly hair in a masculine blue suit and light blue linen shirt. They are smiling warmly and sitting confidently in a black mobility scooter in downtown DC in front of an accessible downtown arts district. On the right is Kenrya Rankin, a dark-skinned Black woman with big, dark brown, kinky hair styled in a twistout, wearing black-rimmed glasses, hot pink lipstick, and a big smile. She is also wearing a shirt with a hot pink, blue, yellow print. The background is white. Below the photos is the Grantmakers in the Arts’ publication and podcast logo in blue and white, “The GIA Reader.” Underneath the logo, the quote from GIA in dark teal text reads, “AI is often framed as either a miracle tool or a looming threat, but what does it actually mean for disabled artists navigating grants, fellowships, and creative partnerships? In this episode, Meier Galblum Haigh and Kenrya Rankin from Disability Culture Lab take over the GIA podcast to unpack the realities of AI, from access tools to authorship, labor, and institutional policy.” Below, dark teal text reads “linktr.ee/DisabilityCultureLab.” In the bottom right corner is page number “1.”

Background is an eggshell-colored square with a red border. At the top is the Disability Culture Lab logo in teal. Below the logo is the Grantmakers in the Arts’ publication and podcast logo in blue and white, “The GIA Reader.” To the right is a square photo of Meier Galblum Haigh, a white trans-masc nonbinary person with short cropped dark curly hair in a masculine blue suit and light blue linen shirt. They are smiling warmly and sitting confidently in a black mobility scooter in downtown DC in front of an accessible downtown arts district. Below is a quote from Meier that reads: “What does it mean to use art to subsidize the bonus of someone like Elon Musk? Especially when our art is meant to express liberation. We need better AI policies and better organizing at this intersection of AI, [disability community], and the arts.” Below, dark teal text reads “Meier Galblum Haigh (they/them), Executive Director.” At the bottom of the page, eggshell text over the red border reads “linktr.ee/DisabilityCultureLab.” In the bottom right corner is page number “2.”

Background is an eggshell-colored square with a red border. At the top is the Disability Culture Lab logo in teal. Below the logo is the Grantmakers in the Arts’ publication and podcast logo in blue and white, “The GIA Reader.” To the right is a square photo of Kenrya Rankin, a dark-skinned Black woman with big, dark brown, kinky hair styled in a twistout, wearing black-rimmed glasses, hot pink lipstick, and a big smile. She is also wearing a shirt with a hot pink, blue, yellow print. The background is white. Below is a quote from Kenrya that reads: “Techno-solutionism is not going to save us. Life has shown us that many of the problems that folks are actually seeking to solve via AI are really failures of the state. Why are folks feeling like they need to use AI to do applications, or to apply for something like Social Security Disability Insurance? Because the administrative burden is just too hard to bear on top of the Crip Tax that we already pay that makes it difficult for us to move through this world, because it’s not built for us.” Below, dark teal text reads “Kenrya Rankin (she/her), Managing Director.” At the bottom of the page, eggshell text over the red border reads “linktr.ee/DisabilityCultureLab.” In the bottom right corner is page number “3.”

value.text: Disability Rising Fellow and Black feminist scholar @drkalyncoghill.blacksky.team recently wrote an op-ed for ESSENCE where they speak to the ways AI amplifies racist attacks against Black nonbinary, agender, and gender-variant people. Read now: linktr.ee/DisabilityCultureLab.

images.alt: Background is a light blue square with a light pink border. At the top is the Disability Culture Lab logo in teal. Below the logo is a square picture of Dr. KáLyn “Kay” Coghill, a Black femme with multi-colored glasses, a tan shirt, and short blonde and pink hair sitting in front of a gold and white bookshelf filled with different books of multiple colors and sizes. Below the photo is the headline of their Essence magazine op-ed: “I’ve Battled Racist Online Trolls For Years. AI Is A Whole Different Beast.” Underneath the headline, the quote from Dr. Coghill in teal text reads, “Where online trolls have long perpetuated racist narratives through words, now they can do the same via images churned out in seconds and spread online even faster. But despite its name, AI can’t shake the fact that it has human DNA. AI mimics what real people, not robots, program it to do and the data it does it with.” Below the quote reads, "Dr. KáLyn “Kay” Coghill (they/them), Disability Rising Fellow" in all caps. At the bottom is teal text that says, “Read Now: linktr.ee/DisabilityCultureLab.”

Every job is AI-proof until the customers run out of money.

The real question is not how much AI can produce, but how many people remain economically necessary to the system.

No skill survives without demand. Skills rarely disappear. Customers do.

A plumber, electrician, contractor, mechanic, or medical specialist may remain indispensable in theory. In practice, their livelihood depends on households with the purchasing power to hire them.

Modern economies run on a simple loop: wages create demand, and demand sustains production.
Artificial intelligence may soon break that loop.

U.S. awaits Iran's response on peace summit as Israel watches
warily

   Summary by Bloomberg AI
   •  The U.S. and a group of regional mediators are discussing the possibility of
      holding high-level peace talks with Iran as soon as Thursday, but are still
      waiting for a response from Tehran.
   •  Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu is concerned Trump might strike
      a deal that falls well short of Israel's objectives, includes significant
      concessions, and limits Israel's ability to conduct strikes against Iran.
   •  The U.S. gave Iran a 15-point proposal for a possible deal to end the war,
      but it's unclear if anyone with authority in Tehran has actually agreed to the
      terms.

By Barak Ravid

(Axios)
The U.S. and a group of regional mediators are discussing the possibility of holding
high-level peace talks with Iran as soon as Thursday, but are still waiting for a
response from Tehran, two sources with knowledge of the discussions tell Axios.
Why it matters: President Trump is interested in winding down the war, but Iran's
chokehold on the Gulf of Hormuz complicates any potential exit strategy. The U.S.
has shared with Israel its 15-point plan to end the war and claimed Iran had
agreed to many of the key points. There's been no tangible evidence of any such
agreement.

   •  Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu is concerned Trump might strike a
      deal that falls well short of Israel's objectives, includes significant
      concessions, and limits Israel's ability to conduct strikes against Iran, two
      Israeli sources say.
   •  A third source said Israeli leaders were skeptical Iran had actually offered the
      concessions the U.S. claimed.

COMPLAINT
In December 2025, the U.S. Department of Homeland Security launched an
immigration enforcement action in Minnesota called "Operation Metro Surge" (the "Surge").

Described by DHS as the largest immigration operation in the Department's history, the Surge
deployed thousands of armed and masked federal agents on the streets of Minnesota.
2) The Surge had a profound effect on all of Minnesota, but its impacts were particularly acute in Hennepin County. It was there during the course of three weeks in January 2026 and at the height of the Surge that federal agents shot and killed two people on public streets and shot and wounded a third person at the threshold of his home. Renee Good and Alex Pretti lost their lives during the Surge, and Julio Cesar Sosa-Celis was wounded, all at the hands of federal agents deployed to Minnesota by Defendants.
3) These shootings are just three examples of the violent actions committed by federal agents in Minnesota during the Surge. Federal agents also carried out illegal stops, sweeps, arrests, and dangerous raids in sensitive public spaces. The Surge created widespread fear among Minnesota residents, both citizens and noncitizens. It caused hundreds of millions of dollars in economic harm. And it flooded Minnesota's federal courts with lawsuits challenging the unlawful detentions that resulted from the operation.

Defendants' refusals to share evidence here did not arise from any case-specific
investigative need. Instead, the breakdown in cooperation followed intervention by senior federal officials who directed that evidence would not be shared with Minnesota authorities, reflecting a
broader policy or practice not to share evidence with Minnesota in Operation Metro Surge use-
of-force investigations.
10)
Faced with unprecedented noncooperation, Plaintiffs submitted formal requests to
Defendant U.S. Department of Homeland Security ("DHS") and Defendant U.S. Department of Justice ("DOJ" or "Justice Department") seeking access to evidence. Those requests invoked
DHS and DOJ's administrative procedures for evaluating requests for agency records and
testimony for use in state proceedings.
11)
Defendants' responses to those requests indeed, by and large, their refusal to
respond at all-confirm that the federal government has adopted a policy and practice of refusing Minnesota authorities access to investigative materials relating to uses of force by federal immigration officers deployed to Minnesota as part of Operation Metro Surge. That policy has
deprived state investigators of timely access to evidence in federal custody that is directly relevant
to their investigations of potential violations of Minnesota criminal law.

Plaintiffs State of Minnesota, by and through its Attorney General Keith Ellison
("State"); Mary F. Moriarty, in her capacity as Hennepin County Attorney ("HCAO"); and Drew
Evans, in his capacity as Superintendent of the Minnesota Bureau of Criminal Apprehension ("BCA"), bring this action against the U.S. Department of Homeland Security and the U.S.
Department of Justice to secure access to the evidence necessary to investigate these shooting

incidents and to ensure that the State of Minnesota can fulfill its sovereign duty to determine
whether federal officers committed crimes within its borders.
13)
Plaintiffs seek declaratory and injunctive relief to set aside Defendants' unlawful
policy of noncooperation and their resulting refusal to comply with the investigative demands of
Minnesota authorities. At stake is not only Plaintiffs' access to evidence central to these shootings
but also a fundamental principle of our constitutional system: that the States retain the sovereign
authority— and responsibility-to investigate crimes committed within their borders.

investigative need. Instead, the breakdown in cooperation followed intervention by senior federal officials who directed that evidence would not be shared with Minnesota authorities, reflecting a
broader policy or practice not to share evidence with Minnesota in Operation Metro Surge use-
of-force investigations.
10)
Faced with unprecedented noncooperation, Plaintiffs submitted formal requests to
Defendant U.S. Department of Homeland Security ("DHS") and Defendant U.S. Department of Justice ("DOJ" or "Justice Department") seeking access to evidence. Those requests invoked
DHS and DOJ's administrative procedures for evaluating requests for agency records and
testimony for use in state proceedings.
11)
Defendants' responses to those requests indeed, by and large, their refusal to
respond at all-confirm that the federal government has adopted a policy and practice of refusing Minnesota authorities access to investigative materials relating to uses of force by federal immigration officers deployed to Minnesota as part of Operation Metro Surge. That policy has
deprived state investigators of timely access to evidence in federal custody that is directly relevant
to their investigations of potential violations of Minnesota criminal law.

Plaintiffs State of Minnesota, by and through its Attorney General Keith Ellison
("State"); Mary F. Moriarty, in her capacity as Hennepin County Attorney ("HCAO"); and Drew
Evans, in his capacity as Superintendent of the Minnesota Bureau of Criminal Apprehension ("BCA"), bring this action against the U.S. Department of Homeland Security and the U.S.
Department of Justice to secure access to the evidence necessary to investigate these shooting
-4-
Case 1:26-cv-01007
Document 1 Filed 03/24/26 Page 5 of 43
incidents and to ensure that the State of Minnesota can fulfill its sovereign duty to determine
whether federal officers committed crimes within its borders.
13)
Plaintiffs seek declaratory and injunctive relief to set aside Defendants' unlawful
policy of noncooperation and their resulting refusal to comply with the investigative demands of
Minnesota authorities. At stake is not only Plaintiffs' access to evidence central to these shootings
but also a fundamental principle of our constitutional system: that the States retain the sovereign
authority— and responsibility-to investigate crimes committed within their borders.

external.description: 💰 📉 HOW WAR REDUCES PURCHASING POWER. ., 🇧🇷 📊 IMPACT ON COST OF LIVING IN BRAZIL (next 1–3 months)  1️⃣ More expensive energy (immediate effect)  The Strait of Hormuz region is responsible for a large part of the world's oil.  👉 When there is risk there:  oil price rises  fuels increase  electricity becomes more expensive   📌 Result: everything gets more expensive (transport, food, production) 2️⃣ Domino effect on food  With expensive fuel:  food transportation becomes more expensive  fertilizers (linked to gas) rise  agricultural production becomes more expensive   👉 Result: 📌 more expensive supermarket worldwide 3️⃣ Global inflation  With energy + food rising:  👉 generalized inflation occurs  And this affects:  salaries (which do not keep up)  retirements  fixed income 📌 Result: 👉 money buys less 4️⃣ Higher interest rates (second impact)  To contain inflation, countries raise interest rates:  👉 credit becomes more expensive 👉 financing weighs more 👉 economy slows down  📌 This reduces purchasing power even more 5️⃣ Instability and fear in the markets  Wars like this generate:  flight of investments  drop in weaker currencies  dollar appreciation   👉 Countries like Brazil suffer more:  📌 imports become more expensive 📌 inflation increases faster 📊 WHO SUFFERS THE MOST  👉 Greater impact on:  energy importing countries  low-income population  retirees (fixed income)   👉 Lesser impact (or even gain) on:  oil producing countries  energy sector 🧠 STRATEGIC READING  👉 This war does not only affect the Middle East  📌 it works like an “invisible global tax”  You don’t see it directly, but you feel it:  at the supermarket  in fuel  in the electricity bill ✅ DIRECT SUMMARY  ✔ war pressures oil and energy ✔ energy makes everything more expensive ✔ inflation rises globally ✔ purchasing power falls 💡 Clear conclusion:  👉 Yes — this war is already affecting people's pockets all over the world 📌 and the longer it lasts, the greater this impact will be 🇧🇷 📊 IMPACT ON COST OF LIVING IN BRAZIL (next 1–3 months)  ⛽ 1️⃣ Fuels (fastest impact)  If oil continues to be pressured:  👉 gasoline and diesel may rise between R$ 0.20 to R$ 0.60 per liter  📌 Direct effect:  more expensive transport  freight rises  everything starts to rise together 🛒 2️⃣ Food (chain effect)  With more expensive transport + fertilizers:  👉 food may rise between 3% and 8%  Most affected:  rice  beans  meats  milk  fruits and vegetables   📌 Practical example: 👉 a purchase of R$ 1,000 can become R$ 1,030 to R$ 1,080 💡 3️⃣ Electricity  If the scenario worsens:  👉 indirect increase of 2% to 5% on the electricity bill  (due to generation costs and global pressure)  💳 4️⃣ Interest rates and credit  If inflation rises:  👉 the Central Bank of Brazil may:  hold back the fall in interest rates  or even raise them again   📌 Result:  more expensive credit  heavier financing 💵 5️⃣ Higher dollar  Crises like this strengthen the dollar:  👉 it may rise to the range of R$ 5.30 to R$ 5.80  📌 Impact:  imported goods more expensive  extra pressure on inflation 📊 TOTAL IMPACT ON THE POCKET  👉 Adding everything up:  📌 cost of living may rise between 5% and 12% in the short term (if the war continues at this level) 🧠 WHO FEELS IT THE MOST  👉 Most impacted:  retirees  wage earners  those living on fixed income   👉 Less impacted:  those with income indexed to inflation  sectors linked to commodities ⚠️ SCENARIOS (important)  🟢 If the war slows down:  smaller impact (3% to 5%)  🔴 If it escalates (Hormuz or energy):  impact may exceed 15% on cost of living 💡 DIRECT SUMMARY  ✔ gasoline may rise up to R$ 0.60 ✔ supermarket may rise up to 8% ✔ electricity up to 5% ✔ dollar under pressure ✔ total impact: up to 12% on cost of living  🧭 CONCLUSION  👉 You are absolutely right: 📌 this war works like a “general price increase” in Brazil  👉 And the most important:  📌 the impact arrives quickly — in weeks, not months

external.description:
このXポスト（https://x.com/i/status/2036485803854221666）は、@Wladislao_Ghcbさんが2026年3月24日16:50頃に投稿したものです。

ポストの内容と背景
引用元：@M_Nishiyokohamaさんが投稿したSmartNews記事の共有  
  記事タイトル：「和尚の読み方は宗派で3通り！『おっさん』と呼ぶ地域や僧侶への正しい敬称マナー」  
  内容は、仏教の宗派によって「和尚」の読み方がおしょう・わじょう・かしょうの3通りあることや、関西圏で親しみを込めて「おっさん（おっさま）」と呼ぶ地域的な習慣、僧侶への正しい敬称マナーを図解で解説したものです。

投稿者の補足コメント（本文そのまま）：  
  「和尚はおしょう以外にもわじょう・かしょうがあり宗派によって読み方が異なりさらに別の宗派では和上も書いてわじょうもあり、寺の主とて呼び方が異なり一般的な呼び方は住職です。また、宗教の最高権威な最高指導者には猊下の敬称もありますね。」

要するに、引用記事の「3通り」をさらに正確に補足しつつ、「和尚」以外の一般的な呼び方や最上級の敬称まで広げた解説ポストです。エンゲージメントはまだゼロなので、純粋に知識共有目的の丁寧なリプライと言えます。

もっと詳しい補足・解説（投稿者の指摘を基に整理）
「和尚」という漢字は同じ字でも宗派で読み方が変わるのが最大のポイントです。信頼できる情報源（Wikipediaや仏教関連サイト）で確認すると、以下の通りです：

| 宗派の例              | 読み方          | 備考 |
|-----------------------|-----------------|------|
| 禅宗（曹洞宗・臨済宗）・浄土宗 | おしょう   | 最も一般的な読み方 |
| 真言宗・法相宗など    | わじょう   | 「和尚」と書く |
| 天台宗・華厳宗など    | かしょう   | 「和尚」と書く |
| 律宗・浄土真宗など    | わじょう   | 「和上」と書いてわじょう（投稿者が指摘した通り） |

投稿者が特に強調した「和上（わじょう）」：これは律宗などで正式に使われる表記で、漢字が少し違うだけで読み方は同じ「わじょう」。記事では触れられていない細かい違いを補っています。
寺の主（住職）の呼び方：宗派に関係なく、日常的に一番無難で正しいのは「住職（じゅうしょく）さん」または「住職様」。  
  「和尚さん」は宗派によって読み方が分かる人向けの呼び方なので、知らない場合は避けた方が無難です。
最高権威の敬称「猊下（げいか）」：これは宗派のトップや非常に高位の僧侶（例：天台宗の座主、真言宗の長者、またはダライ・ラマ猊下など）に対する最上級の敬称。日常ではほぼ使われませんが、公式行事や報道で出てきます。

実生活でのマナーまとめ（記事＋投稿者の補足を統合）
知らないお寺の場合 → 「お坊さん」「住職さん」でOK。宗派を聞けば読み方を合わせられます。
関西圏の「おっさん」 → 親しい地域住民が使う愛称で、失礼ではありませんが、初対面や他地域では絶対に使わない方が良いです（中年男性を指す「オッサン」と混同されやすい）。
正式な場（葬儀・法事など） → 事前に宗派を確認して「○○宗の和尚（正しい読み方）様」と呼ぶか、シンプルに「住職様」で通じます。

このポストは、ネット記事の「3通り」をさらに深掘りしたとても丁寧で正確な補足です。仏教用語の微妙な違いを知っている人が「もっと正確に伝えておこう」と書いた感じですね。興味があれば、元のSmartNews記事も読むと図解で視覚的にわかりやすいですよ！

external.description:
エッセンシャルワーカー（essential worker）とは、直訳で「必要不可欠な労働者」を意味し、社会の基盤を維持するために欠かせない仕事に従事する人々を指します。日本語では「生活必須職従事者」「社会機能維持者」「キーワーカー（key worker）」などとも呼ばれます。

定義の概要
基本的な意味：人々の日常生活や社会インフラを支えるために、緊急事態（パンデミック、災害、ロックダウンなど）でも継続が必要な職業・業務に従事する労働者。
特徴：主に現場作業（テレワークが難しい）が多く、感染リスクや身体的負担が高い場合が多い。コロナ禍で特に注目され、「敬意を払うべき存在」として使われるようになりました。
法的・公式な定義：厳密に一律の定義はなく、国や自治体、状況によって異なります。日本では厚生労働省が「緊急事態宣言時に事業の継続が求められる事業者」と位置づけています。

アメリカでは、CISA（サイバーセキュリティ・インフラセキュリティ庁）の「Essential Critical Infrastructure Workforce」ガイドラインが基盤となり、州ごとに細かく指定されます。CDCもワクチン接種の優先順位付けで活用しました。

主なカテゴリと代表的な職種
エッセンシャルワーカーの範囲は広範ですが、一般的には以下の分野に分けられます（日本・米国で共通する部分が多い）。

医療・公衆衛生
   医師、看護師、介護士、薬剤師、検査技師、救急隊員など。
   病院・介護施設・薬局のスタッフ全般。

治安・安全・防災
   警察官、消防士、自衛官、海上保安官、警備員。
   国境警備や緊急対応関連。

物流・運輸・インフラ
   トラック運転手、鉄道・バス・タクシー運転手、配送員。
   電気・ガス・水道・通信の保守・運用スタッフ。

食料・生活必需品供給
   農業従事者、食品加工・製造、スーパー・小売店員、コンビニスタッフ。
   飲食料品の生産・流通・販売関連。

教育・保育・福祉
   保育士、幼稚園・学校教員、児童福祉施設スタッフ。
   高齢者・障害者支援施設の介護・支援員。

公共サービス・行政
   公務員（行政手続き、廃棄物処理、ごみ収集）、清掃・衛生作業員。
   金融機関の一部（ATM・決済維持）。

その他（製造・建設など）
   医薬品・医療機器製造、建設・メンテナンス（ライフライン関連）、メディア（情報伝達）。

日本では厚生労働省の「緊急事態宣言時に事業継続が求められる事業者」リストが参考になり、医療・介護・物流・小売・行政などが中心です。米国CISAのガイドラインでは14の重要インフラセクター（エネルギー、輸送、食品・農業、水・廃棄物、医療など）が基盤となっています。

背景と注目された理由
コロナ禍での台頭：2020年以降、リモートワークが可能なオフィスワーカーとは対照的に、現場でリスクを負いながら社会を支える人々が「エッセンシャルワーカー」と呼ばれ、感謝のメッセージや優先接種の対象となりました。
ILO（国際労働機関）の視点：生活に不可欠なサービス（医療、食料、小売、保安、運輸など）の従事者を「キーワーカー」と呼び、労働環境改善を提言しています。
課題：感染リスクが高い、長時間労働、低賃金・待遇改善の遅れ、人手不足、偏見・差別など。コロナ期にはPPE（個人防護具）不足も問題になりました。

前回の会話とのつながり
前のポストで議論されたように、自衛隊・警察・消防は「究極のエッセンシャルワーカー」や「国を支える職業」の典型例です。これらは命や社会の秩序を守る最前線で、特に敬意が強調されることが多い一方、医療・介護・物流なども「生活を維持する」重要な役割を担っています。全ての職業に感謝を、というメッセージは、エッセンシャルワーカーの概念を広げた優しい解釈と言えます。

エッセンシャルワーカーは「社会の屋台骨」ですが、明確な境界線は状況次第で変わります。パンデミック時や災害時は特に重要視され、政策（事業継続、優先措置）で支えられる存在です。もし特定の国・分野（例：日本での詳細リストや米国CISAの完全版）についてさらに知りたい場合、教えてください！
